result:
# Static

The radio in the kitchen has been broken for eleven years. My mother keeps it on the counter anyway, between the coffee maker and the ceramic rooster she bought at a yard sale in 1987. She says she likes the way it looks. I think she's waiting for it to work again on its own. She has that kind of faith—not in God exactly, but in the stubbornness of things.

I drove past my elementary school last week. The building is still there but it's a tech company now. Clean glass doors where the wooden ones used to be. I sat in the parking lot for longer than I meant to, watching people in their thirties carry laptops inside, and I tried to remember what it felt like to be six years old and completely certain that the teacher liked you best. That specific warmth. I couldn't quite reach it. It was like trying to remember the exact color of a room you haven't been in for decades—you get close but the shade keeps shifting.

There's a thing that happens around 4 in the afternoon. The light changes. I don't know how to describe it without sounding like someone who has spent too much time alone, but there's a particular quality to it in October especially, something golden and also somehow final, like the day is making a small speech about itself before sitting down. I stop whatever I'm doing when it comes. I stand at whatever window is nearest. I don't know what I'm waiting to understand.

I read once that trees communicate through fungal networks underground. That they send sugars to struggling neighbors. That a forest is less a collection of individuals than a single distributed thing that has learned to look like many. I think about this often. I think about it when I'm in a bad mood and I don't want to. I think about it when I'm sitting next to a stranger on a train and we're both looking at our phones and I wonder what we'd say to each other if we had a different mechanism for it. Something slower and more chemical. Something that didn't require opening your mouth.

My grandfather taught me to play chess when I was eight. He was not a patient man in most situations but with chess he was infinitely patient. He'd wait as long as it took for me to move. He never told me what to do. He believed, I think, that the most important thing was the sitting across from someone and the thinking carefully and the willingness to be wrong in front of them. He died when I was twelve and I still set up the board sometimes. I move both colors. I play honestly for both sides. It goes about how you'd expect.

There's a version of courage that nobody talks about which is just showing up somewhere when you're not sure you're welcome. Not a battlefield. Not a burning building. Just a room with other people in it and the simple act of walking through the door and sitting down and saying with your presence: I believe I belong here. I'm still working on that one.

I made bread this year for the first time. Real bread, with yeast that has to rise and punch down and rise again. It's slow in a way that felt almost rude when I started. I kept wanting to rush it. Then I started just sitting with it in the kitchen, reading or doing nothing, and eventually I understood that the bread didn't need supervision. It just needed time and a warm enough room. I ate the first loaf standing at the counter, pulling pieces off before it had fully cooled. It tasted like something I'd been trying to say for a long time.

I have been wrong about people in both directions. I have thought someone cold and found them later to be one of the most quietly generous people I'd ever known. I have loved someone thoroughly and then discovered the love was mostly made of my own projection—I had been admiring a screen I painted myself. I try to hold both of these errors at the same time when I meet someone new. It makes me a slower judge and probably a better friend. It makes me sometimes look distracted when really I am just reminding myself: you don't know yet.

The broken radio. I should go back to that.

I asked my mother once why she kept it. This was maybe four years ago, after Thanksgiving, when we were cleaning up and I was putting things away and I plugged it in almost as a joke. Nothing. Just that faint electrical smell of an old machine receiving power it can't use.

She thought about it for a while. She washed a dish. She put it in the rack.

She said: *Some things you keep because getting rid of them would mean giving up on a particular version of the house. And I'm not ready to have a different house than this one.*

I didn't say anything. I dried the dish she'd washed and I put it in the cabinet and I thought about all the things I own that are secretly structural in that way. The notebook I haven't written in. The jacket from a trip that went wrong but had three perfect days in it. The phone number in my contacts that belongs to someone who no longer has that number.

We are all, I think, museums of our own hesitations.

The light comes in at 4 o'clock.

The forest keeps its sugar moving through the dark.

My grandfather waits.

I'm still deciding whether to move.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
